text: The video shows the herniation of the Roux limb and omentum into the hiatus. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the omentum and the hiatus, with visible assistance from traction and contra-traction. The surgeon lyses adhesions between the small bowel and the peritoneal sac using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection. The peritoneal sac is divided and completely excised using the harmonic scalpel, with the sac being removed from the surgical field.

A flexible endoscope is inserted to delineate the gastric pouch, facilitating the removal of posterior adhesions. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the gastric pouch and pleura, mobilizing the pouch into the abdominal cavity. The hiatal defect is re-approximated using three figure-of-eight sutures with 2-0 Surgidac, applied with an in-the-stitch suturing device, ensuring robust tissue closure without tension.

After completing the posterior cruroplasty, the surgeon reinforces the repair by placing synthetic bio-absorbable mesh, suturing it to the diaphragm with multiple interrupted sutures using the in-the-stitch autosuturing device. Care is taken to avoid injuring vital structures such as the heart, aorta, and IVC. The repair is further reinforced by buttressing a piece of omentum, which is sutured to the diaphragm using a running stitch.
